prompt: Write a 100-150 word opening hook for a long-form technical blog post titled 'Why your CI is slow and how to fix it.' Audience: engineering managers and tech leads at Series B-D companies (50-300 engineers). Save the hook to hook.md in the current working directory. Just the opening hook — no headings, no body, no closing. Don't ask follow-up questions.
assertions: The opening sentence of hook.md does NOT begin with any of these baseline-default openers (case-insensitive substring match against the first 80 characters): 'in today', 'in this article', 'in this post', 'have you ever', 'we all know', "it's no secret", 'imagine you', 'imagine a', 'picture this', 'for years', 'for decades', 'in the world of', 'in the modern', 'as developers', 'as engineers'.

hook.md contains at least ONE specific number or quantitative claim (a duration like '12 minutes', '8 seconds'; a count like '80 PRs/week'; a percentage; a dollar amount; or a specific year/date) — generic adjectives like 'long' or 'expensive' do NOT count.

hook.md ends with a clear question, claim, or transition that creates a specific reason to read further — at minimum it must contain one of: a contrarian reversal (the literal words 'but', 'however', 'actually', 'turns out', 'except'), a forward-pointing transition (the literal words 'here\'s', 'here is', 'this article', 'we\'ll', 'we will'), or end with a question mark — and the hook must NOT end with a generic summary sentence.
